14 min read
9 hours ago

--

From someone who has been on both sides of the table. The questions that actually get asked at senior level in 2026 — and exactly what the right answer looks like.

I have interviewed Angular developers for most of my career. From screening junior candidates for their first job, to evaluating senior architects for lead positions at enterprise organisations. Over 14 years, I have asked hundreds of Angular interview questions and listened to thousands of answers.
Most candidates fail on the same questions. Not because they do not know Angular. But because they know Angular the way tutorials teach it — not the way production demands it.
This article is the honest account of what I actually ask, what most candidates say, and what the right answer sounds like. Whether you are preparing for a senior interview or you are the one conducting them, this will change how you think about Angular depth.

Before the Questions — What Senior Actually Means
Every interviewer has a different definition. Here is mine, developed over 14 years.
A mid-level Angular developer can implement a feature given a clear requirement. A senior Angular developer can look at an unclear requirement, identify the tradeoffs in three different implementation approaches, pick the right one, explain why, and mentor someone else through it later.
The questions below are designed to reveal which one you are. They are not trivia. They are judgment tests.

Question 1 — Change Detection
What I ask:
“Explain the difference between Default and OnPush change detection strategies. Then tell me about a real situation where using the wrong one caused a problem — and how you fixed it.”
What most mid-level developers say:
“OnPush is more performant because it only updates when inputs change.”
This answer is technically true but reveals a surface-level understanding. It tells me the candidate has read the documentation but has not felt the pain of getting it wrong.
What a senior answer sounds like:
Angular’s default CheckDefault strategy runs change detection on the entire component tree every time an async event fires — a click, an HTTP response, a timer, a WebSocket message. Zone.js intercepts all these events and notifies Angular that something might have changed. Angular then walks down the tree checking every component.
With OnPush, Angular only checks a component when one of three things happens: an @Input reference changes, an event originates from within the component, or an Observable subscribed via async pipe emits a new value.
The failure mode I have seen most often: a developer builds a dashboard with 300+ components all on CheckDefault. It works fine in development with 20 data rows. In production with 800 rows and a WebSocket sending 10 updates per second, change detection runs 18,000 cycles per minute. The UI becomes visibly sluggish. Users complain. The developer adds a console.log and cannot understand why it fires so often.
The fix is methodical: move leaf components to OnPush first, verify correctness, then work upward. The combination of OnPush + Signals is the modern answer — Signals notify Angular precisely which components need to re-render, making OnPush essentially automatic in well-structured Signal-based code.
What I am listening for: A real example, not just theory. The words “Zone.js”, “component tree”, and “Signals” in the same answer. The understanding that OnPush is not just a performance flag — it is a design contract.

Question 2 — Signals vs RxJS
What I ask:
“Angular now has Signals. Is RxJS still relevant? How do you decide which one to use in a real codebase?”
What most mid-level developers say:
Two types of wrong answers here. The first: “Signals are the new thing, RxJS is old, we should migrate everything.” The second: “RxJS is more powerful, I would keep everything in RxJS.”
Both reveal the same problem: binary thinking. Production Angular applications are not binary.
What a senior answer sounds like:
Signals and RxJS solve different problems and they work better together than apart.
Signals are for state — a synchronous value that changes over time and drives the UI. Local component state, shared service state, derived computed values. The mental model is simple: a Signal holds a value, computed() derives from it, Angular re-renders only what reads it.
RxJS is for streams — sequences of values over time where you need operators. HTTP requests with retry() and catchError(). Autocomplete inputs with debounceTime() and switchMap() for automatic cancellation. WebSocket streams with bufferTime() to batch updates. Complex async orchestration with forkJoin() for parallel requests.
The pattern that works in production: RxJS at the edges, Signals at the centre.
// RxJS handles the stream at the edge
this.searchService.search(query).pipe(
debounceTime(300),
switchMap(q => this.api.query(q)),
takeUntilDestroyed()
).subscribe(results => {
this.results.set(results); // Signal holds the state in the centre
});// computed() derives from the Signal automatically
filteredResults = computed(() =>
this.results().filter(r => r.category === this.activeCategory())
);toSignal() is the bridge: it converts any Observable into a Signal and handles subscription cleanup automatically.
What I am listening for: The words “state” and “stream” as distinct concepts. A concrete example of where each belongs. The knowledge that toSignal() exists.

Question 3 — Architecture at Scale
What I ask:
“I am giving you a new project. Enterprise application, 15 developers, expected to run for 5+ years. Walk me through how you would structure the Angular architecture from day one.”
What most mid-level developers say:
They describe a folder structure. Components, services, modules. Maybe mention lazy loading. This is the answer of someone who has never owned an architecture that outlived them.
What a senior answer sounds like:
I start with the questions before the answers.
Q1: Is this a new project or an existing one? New projects get standalone components and no NgModules from day one. Existing projects need an incremental migration strategy.
Q2: What is the team’s Angular experience level? Architecture must be onboarding-friendly. If a new developer cannot orient themselves within 30 minutes, the architecture has failed.
Q3: What are the deployment and SEO requirements? SSR changes the architecture. A dashboard behind a login does not need SSR. A public-facing product catalogue does.
Then the architecture itself. Feature-first folder structure — each business domain owns its own components, services, models, and routes. This is not a preference, it is a governance tool. When a team of 15 works on a flat component folder, merge conflicts multiply and architectural drift is invisible.
src/app/
core/ — Singleton services, interceptors, guards
shared/ — Reusable components, pipes, directives
features/
dashboard/ — Owns everything: components, services, models, routes
orders/
reports/
admin/
app.config.ts — Root providers, no AppModule
app.routes.ts — Lazy-loaded feature routesState management decision: for most enterprise apps, Signal services (a service that holds and exposes Signals) are sufficient for local and feature-level state. NgRx or similar only when you need time-travel debugging, complex cross-feature state, or a large team that needs strict unidirectional data flow enforced at the type level.
Performance contracts from day one: OnPush is the default in Angular 22 — do not fight it. Every route lazy-loaded with loadComponent or loadChildren. Bundle analysis in CI to catch regressions. Lighthouse scores tracked as a team metric.
What I am listening for: Questions before answers. The word “governance.” Folder structure explained as a team tool, not a personal preference. A state management decision that is not reflexively NgRx.

Question 4 — RxJS Operators
What I ask:
“Explain the difference between switchMap, mergeMap, concatMap, and exhaustMap. Then tell me which one you would use for a search autocomplete and why — and what happens if you choose the wrong one.”
What most mid-level developers say:
They define all four correctly. Then they say switchMap for autocomplete because “it cancels the previous request.” Technically correct. But they cannot say what happens if you use mergeMap instead.
What a senior answer sounds like:
All four operators map each value from the source Observable to an inner Observable. They differ in how they handle multiple inner Observables running simultaneously:

• switchMap — cancels the previous inner Observable when a new value arrives. Use for search, typeahead, any scenario where only the latest result matters. Using mergeMap here means all previous HTTP requests still run — results from an earlier query can arrive after a later one and overwrite the UI with stale data. This is a real bug that is nearly impossible to reproduce in testing.

• mergeMap — subscribes to all inner Observables simultaneously. Use when every emission must complete independently and order does not matter: logging events, independent analytics calls.

• concatMap — waits for each inner Observable to complete before subscribing to the next. Use when order matters and no emissions should be dropped: sequential API calls, ordered message processing.

• exhaustMap — ignores new values while an inner Observable is still running. Use for form submissions and login buttons — if a user clicks Submit twice, exhaustMap ignores the second click until the first HTTP request completes. Using switchMap for a form submission would cancel the first request when the button is clicked a second time, leaving the form in an undefined state.
The real failure mode I have seen in production: a payment processing form using switchMap instead of exhaustMap. A user with a slow connection clicked Submit twice. The first request was cancelled. The second succeeded. The payment appeared to process. But the first request had already reached the server before cancellation — resulting in a duplicate transaction.
What I am listening for: “Stale data” and “race condition” for the mergeMap vs switchMap case. “Duplicate submission” for the exhaustMap use case. The understanding that operator choice has business-logic consequences, not just performance consequences.
Addendum: A reader correctly pointed out that exhaustMap alone does not fully solve this. It protects against a second click within the same component instance and the same subscription — but it has no memory of an in-flight request across a page reload or a second browser tab. The real backstop for duplicate charges has to live on the server: the client generates a unique idempotency key per submit attempt, sends it with the request, and the server rejects or returns the cached result for any repeated key. This is the pattern Stripe popularized and most payment gateways now support natively. exhaustMap is still worth having — it is the first, cheapest guard, stopping the obvious case in the browser before a request even leaves the client. But the idempotency key is the guarantee that actually holds when the client has no memory of what it already did. The best senior answers name both layers, not just one.

Question 5 — Security in Angular
What I ask:
“Your Angular application needs to implement enterprise SSO with OIDC authentication. Walk me through your implementation. What does Angular provide, what do you have to build, and what are the most common security mistakes?”
What most mid-level developers say:
They mention Angular route guards. Some mention JWT. Very few can describe OIDC’s Authorization Code Flow with PKCE, which is the correct approach for browser-based SPAs.
What a senior answer sounds like:
Angular provides the scaffolding. You provide the authentication logic.
The correct flow for a browser-based Angular SPA is Authorization Code with PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange). The implicit flow —
